Author: Null Walker
Title: Vibe Coding and Me: A Non-Coder’s Guide to Vibe Coding
Text:
Vibe Coding and Me: A Non-Coder’s Guide to Vibe Coding
Hi everyone,
So this story starts when I saw the announcement for the Bolt hackathon. I thought, well, let’s try something new. I signed up, full of excitement. But that excitement didn’t last long. Their free plan had a daily credit limit of only 150k credits. For the kind of end-to-end applications I was thinking of, the limit was not enough at all. So, I immediately gave up. "This is not for me," I thought, closing the tab.
A few days later, I was cleaning my spam folder. But in between all that, I found one email from Bolt with a voucher code for one month of their premium plan for free. I thought, “Okay, good luck,” and I immediately applied the code. But honestly, the motivation was still zero. The free access was available, but I lacked the motivation to work on anything.
The real change happened one random evening. I joined their Discord server, just to see what other people were building. In one channel, someone from the Bolt team mentioned that they would be giving extra free credits to everyone in the last few days of the hackathon.
That one message was the trigger. Suddenly, I was interested again.
I looked at all the challenges available on the platform. Many of them needed proper planning and a lot of work. But one challenge caught my eye. The topic was: “Build a complete end-to-end product with one prompt.”
At first, it sounds impossible, right? But for me, it also sounded like a smart shortcut. A typical jugaad. If I could figure out how to write a perfect prompt, I would save a lot of time. So I decided to put all my energy into just this one category.
What followed was three days of pure focus. It was proper “vibe coding.” I had no big plan, no documentation. I just sat down with the system and began experimenting with different approaches. I would write a prompt, see the result, change the prompt, and try again. A lot of trial and error. I basically spent three days just talking to the AI, refining my idea with each attempt.
By the end of the hackathon, even I was shocked. I had managed to build six working applications. I couldn’t believe it. I picked the best four and submitted them to the “One-shot Challenge” category.
Honestly, I don’t know if I will win, and that’s okay. The main thing is that I learned so much. I learned how to be a better vibe coder, but with some proper learning and method.
This is my favourite of the six apps I created.
FragranceFolio: A Wikipedia for Perfumes
So, now I will show you how I built it. No complex theory, just a simple breakdown of how I made an entire application with just one single prompt.
My Fight with Rate Limits and One Single Prompt
My idea was to create a Wikipedia of perfumes. For that, the most important thing is data. And we all know that https://www.fragrantica.com
has the biggest database of perfumes on the internet. I planned to get their data, put it in my database, and build an app.
But, as always, there’s a problem. I tried getting the data, but their website has a rate limit. The JSON file I was getting from their requests had no more than 30 results. So I opened up the terminal and tried to play with the parameters using a curl request, but no luck. It was a hard limit.
At this point, I needed to rely on some of my old skills. I wrote a small script to keep iterating the page number and slowly, one by one, collected all the required details of every single perfume available. It took some time, but finally, I had a massive JSON file with all the information. You could say I basically had to hack Fragrantica to get the data for my project.
With the “perfumes.json"
file ready, I added it to my project folder in Bolt. Phase one was complete. I had the database.
Now, for phase two: the recommendation algorithm. A perfume website is not very effective if it doesn’t provide quality recommendations, wouldn’t you agree? But I am no Mark Zuckerberg, and I don’t have a friend like Eduardo Saverin who is going to write a complex algorithm for me. So, I did what every developer does: I went to GitHub.
After some searching, I found this repository: https://github.com/rdemarqui/perfume_recommender
. It looked perfect for what I needed.
I was feeling confident. I created a new prompt in Bolt and put everything in it:
- My
perfumes.json
database. - The link to that GitHub repository for the algorithm.
- The Figma designs I had created for the UI (I know a little bit of designing, so it was no big deal).
I hit enter, and believe me, the entire application was built, and it worked exactly like I wanted it to. I was super happy.
But then I realised my mistake. A big one. The algorithm was fine, but in today’s world, nothing is perfect without AI. I had completely forgotten to add an AI-based recommendation layer.
So, I copied my Gemini API key and planned to use it in Bolt. I would give new instructions to the prompt to create a hybrid system: AI-based recommendations plus the algorithm-based ones.
But here’s the thing with Bolt: I couldn’t just add it to my existing app easily. Since I had failed to create the perfect app in one go, I had to start over in a new window with a new, improvised prompt.
You might think it’s easy, just adding one more instruction. Trust me, I spent the next 8 hours just to master that one prompt. Fine-tuning every single word, making sure the AI logic and the old algorithm worked together, that the database was read correctly, and that the UI from Figma was implemented pixel-perfect. It was a headache, but I was determined to get it right.
Finally, after that 8-hour journey, it worked. One single prompt was now creating the complete, fully functional app.
And the best part? That 8 hours of hard work made me a master of writing prompts. I was now ready to vibe code the next 5 projects in the little time that was remaining.
I know you must be wondering, “Where is this magical prompt?” Let’s just keep it simple for now with a two-way approach: You can comment below, and if enough people ask, I will share it with you. Or just stay tuned, as this is just Day 2 of my random sh!tpost series. More to come!
